sections.title: Genetics & Features
sections.content:
**Mandibulofacial Dysostosis**

**Genetics:**
• TCOF1 gene (most common) - 90%
• POLR1C, POLR1D genes
• Autosomal dominant (60% de novo)
• Variable expressivity

**Classic Features:**
• Malar hypoplasia (underdeveloped cheekbones)
• Downslanting palpebral fissures
• Coloboma of lower eyelid
• Micrognathia/retrognathia
• Ear anomalies (microtia, atresia)
• Conductive hearing loss

**Intelligence:** Usually normal

sections.title: Clinical Manifestations
sections.content:
**Craniofacial:**
• Hypoplastic zygomas and mandible
• Cleft palate (30%)
• Choanal atresia/stenosis

**Eyes:**
• Downslanting palpebral fissures
• Lower lid coloboma
• Absent lower lashes

**Ears:**
• Microtia to anotia
• External auditory canal atresia
• Ossicular anomalies
• Conductive hearing loss (bilateral)

**Airway:**
• Micrognathia with glossoptosis
• Can have severe obstruction (like PRS)
• Difficult intubation

sections.title: Management
sections.content:
**Multidisciplinary Team Essential**

**Airway:**
• Assess at birth
• May need prone positioning, NPA
• Some require tracheostomy
• Mandibular distraction

**Hearing:**
• Early amplification (BAHA)
• Critical for speech development
• Usually not surgical candidates for atresia repair

**Surgical Timeline:**
• Infancy: Airway management, feeding
• 2-3 years: Soft tissue procedures
• 5-7 years: Orbital/malar reconstruction
• Teens: Orthognathic surgery, rhinoplasty
• Adult: Final refinements

**Psychosocial:**
• Support for appearance differences
• Normal intelligence = normal expectations

reviewQuestions.question: A newborn has malar hypoplasia, downslanting palpebral fissures, and bilateral microtia. The parents appear unaffected. What is the inheritance pattern and what percentage of cases are de novo?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Treacher Collins syndrome is autosomal dominant with approximately 60% of cases arising from de novo mutations. Parents may appear unaffected due to variable expressivity or it may be a new mutation.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected treacher collins?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in treacher collins. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of treacher collins. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing treacher collins, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
